Governing body of rugby union in Scotland

The **Scottish Rugby Union** (**SRU**; [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language"): *Aonadh Rugbaidh na h-Alba*) is the [governing body](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") of [rugby union in Scotland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Scotland "Rugby union in Scotland"). Now marketed as **Scottish Rugby**, it is the second-oldest Rugby Union, having been founded in 1873. The SRU oversees the national league system, known as the [Scottish League Championship](/wiki/Scottish_League_Championship "Scottish League Championship"), and the Scottish National teams. The SRU is headed by the President ([Keith Wallace](/wiki/Keith_Wallace_(rugby_union) "Keith Wallace (rugby union)")) and Chairman (John McGuigan), with Mark Dodson acting as the chief executive officer. [Dee Bradbury](/wiki/Dee_Bradbury "Dee Bradbury") became the first female president of a Tier 1 rugby nation upon her appointment on 4 August 2018.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of rugby union in Scotland](/wiki/History_of_rugby_union_in_Scotland "History of rugby union in Scotland")

### 1873–1920s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1873–1920s")]

The **Scottish Football Union** was founded on Monday 3 March 1873 at a meeting held at [Glasgow Academy](/wiki/Glasgow_Academy "Glasgow Academy"), Elmbank Street, Glasgow.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Eight clubs were represented at the foundation, [Glasgow Academicals](/wiki/Glasgow_Academicals_RFC "Glasgow Academicals RFC"); [Edinburgh Academical Football Club](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academical_Football_Club "Edinburgh Academical Football Club"); [West of Scotland F.C.](/wiki/West_of_Scotland_F.C. "West of Scotland F.C."); [University of St Andrews Rugby Football Club](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews_Rugby_Football_Club "University of St Andrews Rugby Football Club"); [Royal High School FP](/wiki/Royal_High_School_(Edinburgh)#Sports_and_games "Royal High School (Edinburgh)"); [Merchistonians](/wiki/Merchistonian_FC "Merchistonian FC"); [Edinburgh University RFC](/wiki/Edinburgh_University_RFC "Edinburgh University RFC"); and [Glasgow University](/wiki/Glasgow_University_Rugby_Football_Club "Glasgow University Rugby Football Club"). Five of these clubs were, at the time of founding the Scottish Football Union, already members of the previously instituted [Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"). Although the RFU now represents exclusively English clubs, in its first few years it had members from outside England, there being no other national union. West of Scotland, Glasgow Academicals and [Edinburgh University](/wiki/Edinburgh_University_RFC "Edinburgh University RFC") had joined the RFU in 1871 and Edinburgh Academicals and Royal High School FP had joined in 1872. These five renounced membership of the RFU to join the SFU.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The SFU was a founding member of the one and only International Rugby Football Board, now known as [World Rugby](/wiki/World_Rugby "World Rugby"), in 1886 with [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Rugby_Football_Union "Irish Rugby Football Union") and [Wales](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union"). (England refused to join until 1890.)

In 1924 the SFU changed its name to become the Scottish Rugby Union.[[3]](#cite_note-1925GS-3) International games were played at [Inverleith](/wiki/Inverleith_Sports_Ground "Inverleith Sports Ground") from 1899 to 1925 when Murrayfield was opened.

### 1990s–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1990s–present")]

The SRU owns [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") which is the main home ground of the [Scottish national team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team"), though in 2004 international rugby games were played at [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park") in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") and [McDiarmid Park](/wiki/McDiarmid_Park "McDiarmid Park") in [Perth](/wiki/Perth,_Scotland "Perth, Scotland"), as part of the SRU's campaign to reach out to new audiences outside the traditional rugby areas.

When the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") (now replaced by the [European Rugby Champions Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Champions_Cup "European Rugby Champions Cup")) was suggested SRU officials were concerned that Scottish club sides could not compete against the best teams from France and England and that centrally funded so-called 'super-district' teams might do better.

The four traditional districts—the South (renamed [Border Reivers](/wiki/Border_Reivers_(rugby_union) "Border Reivers (rugby union)")), Edinburgh, Glasgow and the North & Midlands (rebranded as [Caledonia Reds](/wiki/Caledonia_Reds "Caledonia Reds"))—were given the go-ahead to take part in Europe. For the first two seasons, players were still released to play for their clubs in domestic competition, but eventually the districts became full-time operations.

Then financial difficulties – the SRU's high debt, partly as a result of the redevelopment of [Murrayfield](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") – called for retrenchment. After two seasons, financial difficulties forced the SRU to merge the four teams into two. Edinburgh merged with the Border Reivers to form a team to be known as Edinburgh Reivers. Glasgow merged with Caledonian to form a team to be known as Glasgow Caledonian.

[The Borders](/wiki/Border_Reivers_(rugby) "Border Reivers (rugby)") was resurrected in 2002 and joined the second season of the Celtic League, now known as the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship"). As a consequence Edinburgh Reivers became simply [Edinburgh Rugby](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") and Glasgow became Glasgow Rugby.[[4]](#cite_note-AnnRep2003-4) In 2005, all three teams adopted new names. The Borders readopted the name Border Reivers; Edinburgh became Edinburgh Gunners, but would revert to Edinburgh in 2006 due to [Arsenal F.C.](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") owning the "Gunners" trademark;[[5]](#cite_note-5) and Glasgow became [Glasgow Warriors](/wiki/Glasgow_Warriors "Glasgow Warriors"). Furthermore, the SRU planned to have a world-class rugby side for each city or large town in Scotland, when financial circumstances permitted.[[6]](#cite_note-dictators-6)

In 2007, The Borders team was disbanded yet again as a result of continuing financial difficulties. In the same year, the SRU began organising the [Scotland Sevens](/wiki/Scotland_Sevens "Scotland Sevens"), first held in Edinburgh and later in Glasgow. For several years, it was the final event in the annual [Sevens World Series](/wiki/SVNS "SVNS"), but that distinction now belongs to Madrid.

On 21 November 2009 Scotland beat Australia 9–8 after 17 attempts in 27 years.

### Centenary celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Centenary celebrations")]

The SRU celebrated its centenary in 1973 with a number of events. Among these was the [1973 International Seven-A-Side Tournament](/wiki/1973_International_Seven-A-Side_Tournament "1973 International Seven-A-Side Tournament"), the first sevens tournament to have national representative sides. The programme for that event also sported the new coat of arms of the SRU that was granted by the [Lord Lyon King of Arms](/wiki/Lord_Lyon_King_of_Arms "Lord Lyon King of Arms") on 28 February 1973, for the centenary season. The coat of arms is still in use today, but in the main the SRU use the commercial thistle logo on jerseys and stationery. The coat of arms has the motto *"Non Sine Gloria"*, meaning *"Not Without Glory"*.

## Domestic Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Domestic Rugby")]

See also: [Scottish rugby union system](/wiki/Scottish_rugby_union_system "Scottish rugby union system") and [Rugby union in Scotland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Scotland "Rugby union in Scotland")

The SRU oversees the national league system, known as the [Scottish League Championship](/wiki/Scottish_League_Championship "Scottish League Championship"), and consisting of:

* a [Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership_(rugby) "Scottish Premiership (rugby)") of 20 teams across 2 divisions.
* a [National Leagues](/wiki/Scottish_Championship_Leagues "Scottish Championship Leagues") of 20 teams across 2 regional divisions.
* [Regional Leagues](/wiki/Scottish_Regional_Leagues "Scottish Regional Leagues") of 150 clubs in 18 divisions across three regions

It also oversees the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup_(rugby_union) "Scottish Cup (rugby union)"). It is not directly responsible for local, university or 2nd XV leagues.

### Women's & Girls Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Women's & Girls Rugby")]

See also [Scottish Women's Rugby](/wiki/Scottish_Women%27s_Rugby "Scottish Women's Rugby").

Since the [Scottish Women's Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Women%27s_Rugby_Union "Scottish Women's Rugby Union") merged with Scottish Rugby in 2009[[7]](#cite_note-7) the governing body also oversees Women's Fixtures.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

* [Tennent's Women's Premier League 2018–19](http://www.scottishrugby.org/fixtures-and-results?competition=2)[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* [Tennent's Women's National League 1 2018–19](http://www.scottishrugby.org/fixtures-and-results?competition=6)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [Tennent's Women's National League 2 2018–19](http://www.scottishrugby.org/fixtures-and-results?competition=7)[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [Tennent's Women's North League 2018-19](http://www.scottishrugby.org/fixtures-and-results?competition=42)
* [Women's National Development League 2018-19](http://www.scottishrugby.org/fixtures-and-results?competition=204)

A National Cup competition:

* Sarah Beaney Cup 2015–16[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* BT Women's Bowl[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Regional Cup Competition:

* Donna Kennedy Cup[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## National teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: National teams")]

Main articles: [Scotland national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") and [Scotland national rugby sevens team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_sevens_team "Scotland national rugby sevens team")

The SRU oversees Scotland's national teams. The most prominent team is the [Scotland national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team"), which competes in the Six Nations tournament every year and in the Rugby World Cup every four years. The SRU also oversees the [Scotland national sevens team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_sevens_team "Scotland national rugby sevens team"), which competes every year in the [World Rugby Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "World Rugby Sevens Series"). And the SRU oversees the Scotland national women's rugby team.

The head coach of the Scotland national rugby union team is [Gregor Townsend](/wiki/Gregor_Townsend "Gregor Townsend"), who began in June 2017.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Scott Johnson](/wiki/Scott_Johnson_(rugby_coach) "Scott Johnson (rugby coach)") is Director of Rugby.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The head coach of the Scottish national women's rugby team is [Shade Munro](/wiki/Shade_Munro "Shade Munro").

## North American initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: North American initiatives")]

On 28 March 2019, the SRU announced that it had taken a minority stake in [Old Glory DC](/wiki/Old_Glory_DC "Old Glory DC"), a team set to begin play in [Major League Rugby](/wiki/Major_League_Rugby "Major League Rugby") (MLR), the current top level of the sport in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), in 2020.[[17]](#cite_note-17) As MLR operates on a "single-entity" business model similar to the two countries' [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer"), with the league owning all teams and the team operators being league shareholders,[[18]](#cite_note-18) this effectively meant that the SRU had taken a small stake in MLR itself.

## Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Rugby_Union&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personnel")]

Main articles: [President of the Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/President_of_the_Scottish_Rugby_Union "President of the Scottish Rugby Union") and [List of Scottish Rugby Union Presidents](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_Rugby_Union_Presidents "List of Scottish Rugby Union Presidents")

The current president is [Keith Wallace](/wiki/Keith_Wallace_(rugby_union) "Keith Wallace (rugby union)"), who succeeded [Colin Rigby](/wiki/Colin_Rigby "Colin Rigby") in 2024. John McGuigan was appointed chair in May 2023.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The Chief Executive Officer is Mark Dodson.[[20]](#cite_note-Scotsman_10Aug2015-20)

In May 2014, it was announced that [Sheila Begbie](/wiki/Sheila_Begbie "Sheila Begbie") had been appointed to the newly created post of Head of Women's Rugby, reporting directly to the Director of Rugby and she was due to commence this role in August 2014.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
